does: This paper introduces Prompt Infection, a novel attack vector in multi-agent LLM systems where malicious prompts self-replicate across interconnected agents similarly to computer viruses. The authors demonstrate through extensive experiments that multi-agent systems are highly vulnerable to this attack even when agents do not publicly share all communications, with threats ranging from data theft and scams to misinformation and system-wide disruption. The paper shows that prompt injection attacks are far more dangerous when propagating across multiple coordinated LLM agents than in single-agent contexts, and proposes LLM Tagging as a defense mechanism that, when combined with existing safeguards, significantly mitigates the attack's effectiveness.
connects: This work extends prior research on prompt injection attacks, which have been studied extensively as vulnerabilities in single-agent LLM applications where external malicious content tricks models into executing unintended actions. The paper builds on the growing literature documenting safety risks in LLMs by identifying a new threat surface: the interconnections between multiple LLM agents in multi-agent systems. It responds to the gap in safety research that has largely focused on isolated agent vulnerabilities, drawing on network-based attack concepts like viral propagation to model how malicious prompts exploit agent-to-agent communication channels and reasoning processes.
enables: This work opens investigation into defenses for multi-agent LLM systems, motivating research into tagging mechanisms and other architectural safeguards that can distinguish trusted internal communications from potentially compromised external inputs. It points toward future work on designing LLM systems with improved isolation, verification, and trust mechanisms between agents, as well as detection systems for identifying propagating malicious prompts. The framework of prompt infection as a replicating attack class enables development of multi-agent specific safety protocols and may inform broader questions about how coordinated LLM systems should be designed to prevent emergent vulnerabilities from exploiting communication patterns.
